content.text: What Is a Single Agent?
A
single agent

is one LLM inference loop with one context window, one set of tools, and one goal to pursue. All reasoning, planning, and action selection happens within that single loop. There are no sub-agents, no delegation, and no parallel inference calls.
Single agents are the
default architecture

for LLM agents. Before reaching for a multi-agent system, consider whether a single agent with a well-designed tool set and clear goal specification can solve the problem. For most tasks, it can — and the simplicity reduces cost, latency, and debugging time significantly.
Single Agent Architecture
A single agent consists of four components:
System prompt
: the agent's role, constraints, tool usage guidelines, and stopping criterion.
Tool set
: the actions the agent can take. Typically 3–10 tools covering read, compute, write, and control operations.
Message history
: the accumulating context window containing the conversation, tool calls, and tool results.
Loop controller
: the host-side code that drives the Reason → Act → Observe → Repeat cycle.
When to Use a Single Agent
A single agent is the right choice when:
Criterion
Single agent is appropriate when...
Reconsider if...
All observations fit comfortably in the context window
Tool results are so large they exhaust the window within 5 iterations
The task has a linear or branching sequence of steps
The task has multiple independent sub-tasks that could run in parallel
Each step depends on the previous result
Sub-tasks are fully independent and could benefit from parallelism
Sequential tool calls fit within the allowed response time
Parallelism is required to meet strict latency targets
A single failure is recoverable within one loop
One agent's error would cascade into other agents' work

Most customer support agents, coding assistants, research helpers, and data analysis agents are well-served by a single agent architecture. Adding a second agent only makes sense when the task genuinely requires parallel execution or when context window limits prevent a single agent from handling the full scope.
Single Agent Strengths
Simple debugging
: the full message history is one list. Every decision the agent made, and why, is visible in order. There is no inter-agent communication to trace.
Lower latency
: no inter-agent overhead, no serialisation of results between agents, no coordination protocol. The loop runs as fast as the API allows.
Coherent context
: one agent has the full history. It cannot 'forget' something another agent observed, because everything is in the same context window.
Lower cost
: one context means one set of input tokens per round. Multi-agent systems duplicate parts of the context across agents, inflating total token spend.
Easier to test
: a deterministic input produces a deterministic sequence of tool calls (for the same model version). Unit-testing a single agent is straightforward.
Single Agent Limitations
Single agents hit hard limits at scale:
Context window ceiling
: the context window is finite. A task that requires 50 tool calls, each returning 2,000 tokens of results, will exhaust even a 200,000-token window.
No parallelism
: tool calls within one turn can be parallelised, but the agent cannot run two independent reasoning threads simultaneously.
Single point of failure
: if the agent makes a wrong decision at step 3, every subsequent step is built on that mistake. There is no second agent to provide a check.
Skill breadth vs. depth
: a single agent with many tools may be less effective than two specialised agents, each with a focused tool set and role-specific system prompt.
System Prompt Design for Single Agents
The system prompt is the single agent's constitution. It tells the agent its
role
, its
tools
, its
constraints
, and its
stopping criterion

. A well-designed system prompt produces consistent, predictable behaviour across many different user inputs. A poorly designed one produces inconsistent tool use, incomplete answers, and runaway loops.
The four essential sections of a single-agent system prompt:
Role declaration
: one sentence. What is this agent and what domain does it cover?
Tool usage guidelines
: for each tool, when to use it and when NOT to use it. Be explicit.
Reasoning requirement
: instruct the agent to write a brief Thought before each action.
Stopping criterion
: exactly which tool signals completion, and what to put in it.

SYSTEM_TEMPLATE = """
You are a {role} for {domain}.

## Tools
{tool_descriptions}

## Process
Before each action, write:
Thought: <what you know | what you still need | which tool and why>

## Completion
When the task is fully resolved, call task_complete with a clear, direct answer.
When the task cannot be resolved (missing data, out of scope, impossible),
call task_failed with an explanation.
Never stop without calling one of these two tools.

## Constraints
{constraints}
"""

# Example: customer support agent
SYSTEM = SYSTEM_TEMPLATE.format(
    role="customer support agent",
    domain="an e-commerce platform",
    tool_descriptions=(
        "- get_order(order_id): look up order status, items, and ETA\n"
        "- update_order_status(order_id, status): cancel orders ONLY — not other status changes\n"
        "- search_faq(query): search the help centre for policy answers\n"
        "- task_complete(answer): call with a full answer when the customer is satisfied\n"
        "- task_failed(reason): call if the issue is outside your scope or impossible to resolve"
    ),
    constraints=(
        "- Do not process refunds — direct to the refund team.\n"
        "- Do not reveal internal order IDs other than those the customer mentions.\n"
        "- Confirm order ID before cancellation — ask the customer to provide it explicitly."
    ),
)
The tool descriptions in the system prompt must match the
TOOLS

list passed to the API. If a tool appears in one and not the other, the agent will either hallucinate tool calls (if described in the prompt but not listed) or underuse a tool (if listed but not described in the prompt).
Token Budget and Context Lifecycle
A single agent's context window is its working memory. It accumulates Thoughts, tool calls, and tool results with every iteration. The total input token count for a loop of
N
iterations with an initial context of
C₀
tokens and
Δ
tokens of growth per iteration is:

For a 10-iteration agent with C₀ = 2,000 and Δ = 800 tokens per round, total input = 20,000 + 36,000 = 56,000 tokens. At Claude Haiku pricing (≈ $0.25 / 1M tokens), that is $0.014 per run. At 1,000 runs per day, $14 / day — so iteration count and Δ have a direct dollar cost.
Context pressure source
Typical Δ per round
Mitigation
100–300 tokens
No action needed
300–800 tokens
Trim to 5 results, 150 chars per snippet
500–2,000 tokens
Return exit code + last 20 lines only
Summarise before returning; never return raw
Extract relevant paragraphs only
200–500 tokens
Acceptable; do not suppress — they guide behaviour
Testing a Single Agent
Testing a single agent requires covering four distinct code paths — not just the happy path. The agent's behaviour at each loop exit point must be verified independently.
Test case
Input condition
Expected outcome
User message that tool set can fully answer
status == "complete"; answer is non-empty
User message that no tool can resolve
status == "failed"; reason explains why
Task partially solvable; model must acknowledge limit
status in ("failed", "complete"); answer notes limitation
Task designed to take more steps than the cap
status == "max_iterations"; no crash
Force a mock to return unknown tool name
Error JSON returned; model recovers or calls task_failed
Tool returns empty results list
Model tries alternative or calls task_failed; no infinite retry

import json
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock

def make_mock_response(tool_name: str, tool_input: dict, response_id: str = "tu_001"):
    """Build a mock Anthropic API response that calls one tool."""
    block = MagicMock()
    block.type = "tool_use"
    block.name = tool_name
    block.input = tool_input
    block.id = response_id

    resp = MagicMock()
    resp.content = [block]
    resp.usage = MagicMock(input_tokens=500)
    return resp

def make_final_response(text: str = ""):
    """Build a mock response with no tool calls (agent stuck or done)."""
    block = MagicMock()
    block.type = "text"
    block.text = text

    resp = MagicMock()
    resp.content = [block]
    resp.usage = MagicMock(input_tokens=200)
    return resp

# Test: happy path — one search, then task_complete
def test_happy_path():
    search_resp = make_mock_response("search", {"query": "Paris population"}, "tu_001")
    complete_resp = make_mock_response(
        "task_complete", {"answer": "Paris has 2.1M residents."}, "tu_002"
    )

    with patch("anthropic.Anthropic") as MockClient:
        mock_client = MockClient.return_value
        mock_client.messages.create.side_effect = [search_resp, complete_resp]

        # run_agent returns a dict — check status and answer
        result = run_agent(mock_client, SYSTEM, TOOLS, DISPATCH, "How many people live in Paris?")
        assert result["status"] == "complete"
        assert "Paris" in result["answer"]
        assert result["iterations"] == 2
        print("PASS test_happy_path")

# Test: max iterations cap
def test_max_iterations():
    # Every response calls 'search' — never reaches task_complete
    looping_resp = make_mock_response("search", {"query": "Paris"}, "tu_loop")

    with patch("anthropic.Anthropic") as MockClient:
        mock_client = MockClient.return_value
        mock_client.messages.create.return_value = looping_resp

        result = run_agent(mock_client, SYSTEM, TOOLS, DISPATCH, "Paris?", max_iterations=3)
        assert result["status"] == "max_iterations"
        assert result["iterations"] == 3
        print("PASS test_max_iterations")
Unit tests that mock the Anthropic API run without network calls and without spending tokens. Cover all six exit states in the table above before deploying a single agent in production.
Observability: Logging the Agent's Decisions

A single agent's full message history is available after every run. Logging it is the most important observability tool: every Thought the agent wrote, every tool call it made, and every result it received is already in
messages
. Extract it, serialize it, and store it alongside the final outcome.

The step-by-step log reveals exactly where the agent diverged from the expected path. When an agent produces a wrong answer, the log usually shows either a malformed Thought that chose the wrong tool, or an observation that the agent misread. Both are fixable: the first by refining the system prompt, the second by improving the tool's output format.
Start with a single agent and add complexity only when you hit a concrete limit.

The most common mistake in agent system design is reaching for multi-agent architectures before a single agent has been given a fair trial. A single agent with a good system prompt, a clean tool set, and a proper loop controller will handle the majority of agentic tasks more cheaply, faster, and with less engineering overhead than a distributed multi-agent system.
